skill.name: create-pr
skill.description: Creates GitHub pull requests by guiding status review, explicit staging, commit creation, branch push, and GitHub PR creation.
skill.summary: Guides a complete GitHub pull request workflow from local review to PR URL.
skill.author: 0tarof

security_audit.summary: Static external command findings are mostly true positives because the skill intentionally runs git and GitHub CLI commands. The hardcoded URL, weak cryptography, and high-entropy alerts are false positives in readable Markdown content, but the workflow still has medium operational risk because it can commit, push, create PRs, and run repository-defined checks.

medium_findings.title: External Git and GitHub Commands Can Modify Repositories
medium_findings.description: The external command findings are true positives. The skill intentionally guides git status, diff, add, commit, push, and gh pr create commands. This is legitimate for a PR workflow, but it can change local repository state and publish branches or pull requests.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines contain explicit git and GitHub CLI workflow commands. The intent is legitimate, but the commands have real side effects on repositories and remote GitHub state.

medium_findings.title: Repository-Defined Checks May Execute Untrusted Code
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs the agent to read project requirements and run tests, linters, build steps, or repository-specific scripts before creating a PR. This is useful, but in an untrusted repository those checks can execute arbitrary project code.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file clearly directs execution of project-specific validation commands. No malicious command is shown, but repository-controlled scripts are a known execution risk.

low_findings.title: Shell Command Substitution Uses Quoted Heredoc Templates
low_findings.description: The shell command substitution findings are present, but the examples use quoted heredoc delimiters to build commit and PR bodies. No evidence shows direct execution of user-provided body content, so this is a low-risk workflow pattern rather than command injection.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command substitution is visible, but the heredoc delimiter is single-quoted in both examples. That context makes shell expansion inside the body unlikely unless the template is altered.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URL Is Benign Attribution Text
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URL findings point to Claude Code attribution text in commit and PR templates. No evidence shows data exfiltration, credential transfer, or unauthorized network requests to that URL.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Both URL locations are static attribution lines. The only active network action is GitHub PR creation through the expected gh command.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography and Entropy Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptographic algorithm alert at the description line and the high-entropy alert at the file start do not match the readable Markdown content. No evidence found of cryptography, encoded payloads, encrypted content, or obfuscation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are plain frontmatter and a Japanese description. Manual review found no weak crypto API, binary blob, encrypted payload, or hidden instruction there.

dangerous_patterns.title: Git State-Changing Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes git add, git commit, and git push examples. These commands are expected for this skill, but they can permanently change repository history and publish a branch.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The exact git commands appear in Bash snippets. Their side effects are inherent to the intended pull request workflow.

dangerous_patterns.title: GitHub CLI Network Publication
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill uses gh pr create to create a pull request. This publishes PR metadata and branch context to GitHub through the configured account.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The GitHub CLI command is explicit. It is expected behavior, but it is still a network publication action.

dangerous_patterns.title: Project Script Execution Guidance
dangerous_patterns.description: The workflow tells the agent to run tests, linters, builds, and repository-specific validation scripts. This can execute code from the checked-out repository.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The instructions explicitly require project validation steps before progressing. The exact commands depend on repository content, so risk depends on trust in the repository.

content.value_statement: Preparing pull requests requires status checks, clean commits, branch pushes, and clear PR descriptions. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through that workflow with explicit staging and review steps.
actual_capabilities: Reviews git status, diffs, and recent commits before making a PR.
Stages files with explicit paths instead of broad add commands.
Creates commit messages that follow the repository style.
Pushes the current branch to the origin remote.
Creates a GitHub pull request with a template or fallback body.
Returns the created pull request URL to the user.
limitations: Requires git and GitHub CLI to be installed and authenticated.
Can only create PRs in repositories where the user has permission.
May run repository scripts that should be trusted before execution.
The original workflow expects Japanese communication with the user.

use_cases.title: Prepare a solo developer PR
use_cases.description: Turn local changes into a reviewed commit and GitHub pull request with a clear summary.

use_cases.title: Standardize team contributions
use_cases.description: Follow repository checks, commit style, and pull request templates before opening review.

use_cases.title: Reduce maintainer review friction
use_cases.description: Create pull requests that include tested changes, focused staging, and useful PR descriptions.

prompt_templates.title: Create a basic PR
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner workflow for current local changes.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a pull request for my current changes. Review the status first, then guide me through commit, push, and PR creation.

prompt_templates.title: Run required checks first
prompt_templates.scenario: Use repository instructions before opening review.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my changed files, read the repository requirements, run the required checks, and create a pull request only if they pass.

prompt_templates.title: Stage selected files only
prompt_templates.scenario: Create a focused PR from specific paths.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a pull request using only these files: src/example.ts and tests/example.test.ts. Do not stage unrelated changes.

prompt_templates.title: Match repository conventions
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced workflow with commit and PR style matching.
prompt_templates.prompt: Prepare a pull request that matches recent commit style, follows the PR template, includes test results, and stops if any check fails.

output_examples.input: Create a PR for my current changes.
output_examples.output: The assistant reviews changed files, summarizes the diff, confirms staged paths, creates a commit, pushes the branch, and returns the PR URL.

output_examples.input: Open a PR after running the project checks.
output_examples.output: The assistant reports which checks ran, whether they passed, the commit title used, and the created pull request link.

output_examples.input: Create a PR using only the documentation updates.
output_examples.output: The assistant stages only the requested documentation files, commits them, creates a focused PR, and notes excluded changes.

best_practices: Review the diff before allowing commits or pushes.
Use explicit file paths when staging changes.
Run required checks before creating the pull request.
anti_patterns: Do not stage all files without reviewing unrelated changes.
Do not create a pull request after failed tests or builds.
Do not run repository scripts from untrusted projects without approval.

faq.question: Does this skill create the pull request automatically?
faq.answer: Yes. It guides the agent through commit, push, and GitHub PR creation when the required tools and permissions are available.

faq.question: Which tools does it need?
faq.answer: It needs git for local repository actions and GitHub CLI for creating the pull request.

faq.question: Does it stage every changed file?
faq.answer: No. The workflow requires explicit file paths and warns against broad staging commands.

faq.question: Can it use an existing pull request template?
faq.answer: Yes. It checks for a repository pull request template and uses it when present.

faq.question: Is it safe for untrusted repositories?
faq.answer: Use caution. Repository checks and scripts can execute project code, so review commands before running them.

faq.question: Does it support Claude Code attribution?
faq.answer: Yes. The included templates add Claude Code attribution to commits and pull request bodies.
